speaker: interviewer
text: Being in Auschwitz, what time of year and what year was it you were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to Auschwitz in-- I wouldn't know exact date, but in September, 1944. And I must have been there about six weeks all together. And because there was such an influx when I came to Auschwitz, I never got a number, which I would like to have. I was never tattooed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me what was your overall impression of Auschwitz? If somebody asked you that question.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was utterly, utterly unreal. It is like if you see a spooky movie, and, and, and I had the feeling at times that I'm just looking in, that it is not happening, that it is a nightmare or, or, or, or some movie or-- it was just so utterly, utterly unreal. Because the people-- I mean, all the time somebody came into the barracks, and we had to strip naked, and we had to stand for hours naked, and the band was going around playing. And when we went to the latrines, the, the guys that were supposed to be murderers-- because we were told if it's a, a, a red triangle or whatever-- so they were in charge of us. And there we were sitting on the latrines with the maggots in, in-- lower-- ugh. Looking down at the maggots, I mean, you know, you always were like, I hope I won't fall in. And there you sat once a day. And you had ten women in front of you saying, hurry up, hurry up. And then the, the guy who is the murderer in his pajama with a long, long whip, was running around whipping us so that we-- whatever we had to do, we'd do it quicker.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, it, it was just totally nutty. It was totally-- I think the only thing they had organized well was the killing machine. Otherwise, it just nothing made sense in that place. I was so glad to get out of it. I mean, once you are there, I mean, you know you can't run away. There were a few people who ran away from Auschwitz, but, but it, it would have been impossible. Impossible. We had no hair. We had no clothes. I mean, how could-- how could you escape? And who told you about the gas chambers or did you work that out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, no. When, when-- eventually, some of the boys from Theresienstadt-- not the ones we wanted to meet, but some others we knew-- friends. They-- I don't know-- they had some jobs in the-- in Auschwitz. And they could walk around. And they came into, into our barracks. What position, what they had to do, take messages or something. And so we learned all-- we learned-- we understood exactly what is going on. This is why I knew that the women and children, they were all gassed straight away. They went straight from the train station into, into the gas chamber.

speaker: interviewee
text: We knew what is going on, but we-- nobody knew will we stay. I mean, because we were not gassed the first day, it looked sort of promising that, you know-- they could have done it straight away. Why keep us there? So, so all you did, you just lived from minute to minute, hoping that they won't let you stand again outside in the cold. And, and when you went down-- when I went down those three floors, by the time I came down, I had one big shoe and one little shoe.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there was so much mud-- I don't know why it was so muddy-- that you could hardly pull your feet out of the mud. Well, it was a nightmare.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you frightened that you were going to be gassed? Were you-- or were you--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I never thought about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: No?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never thought about it. I, I, I mean, probably for me, being in that situation made it easier because I didn't so much worry about Goldie. I had to worry a little bit about myself. But I never-- I never worried that I'd die. I still-- it doesn't matter to me. It's not terribly important.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you with anybody that you knew? Did you make friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes. That was so important. All right through the whole camp, it was a group of us that were-- that were very good friends, and we are still-- whoever is alive-- we are still writing all over the world. And I think that was the most-- that kept you really alive, to, to have somebody, because there were a lot of Polish women, and we had nothing in common. I, I, I was very nasty. I was horrible. I didn't know that they-- they were so primitive, but I didn't know it is because they had no education. They had-- they lived in ghettos so long, I wouldn't have known. I just hated them. They behaved like savages.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we Czech girls, we, we were sort of a group. We were trying always, how, how whatever happened, to stay together, which we succeeded. Tell me about the further selections that you went through.

speaker: interviewee
text: That, that was the only time with Mengele that, that I was-- all, all we did was stand appell, and then we all went in. Nobody was sick in our hut because we were all in, in good condition. And I had no more selection. One day we were, again-- I don't know-- herded out, always in fives. And we were given clothes, because we only had this one piece. So we were given-- they looked like ski shoes, but they were made of, of cotton or something and with a wooden sole. And a warm coat and a flannel dress with flowers. And I think even, even some bloomers, huge bloomers. The women who handed it out, they were the Slovaks or the Poles and they were so cruel. They never cared what they gave you. They gave the biggest woman the little garment. And so sometimes we swapped things. Anyhow, we got this. And we were all dressed. And then we stood, again, in some wide road and, and then we were put in the train and we were out of Auschwitz. And it took-- I don't-- I can't quite remember. I think it took only [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] two days-- three days-- and all at once, we were in Germany. It was called Freiberg. It was near Dresden. And there was this enormous factory, and it was-- previously it was, I think, the biggest in Europe. A porcelain factory. It was [INAUDIBLE]. And that factory has been converted to make the wings of the V1 bomb. And first we had-- we lived in the-- in the factory, which was, in a way, better because it was-- we didn't have to go outdoors. And eventually we had-- we had to go live in some army barracks and it was very hard to-- night shift-- to walk again-- it was so cold-- to the barracks, and so we worked 12-hour shifts. I was drilling the holes for the riveting. And they were very small wings and we had no idea what it was. All we knew that they were only the wings, and that eventually we found out the wings have to get together with the other parts. But the other parts were not made in Freiberg. They had to be shifted away. And there were a lot of men working in that factory. And I can't explain what it was. They were German-speaking engineers, and they were Communists. How they-- for what reason-- like they used us as slave labor. They used them, too, but they were not-- they were not prisoners. They were civilians who did important jobs on that V1 bomb with, with the prisoners. And when we came first to, to the factory, they couldn't believe their eyes. These women in awful outfits. No hair. They thought that we some savages. And then eventually they started talking to us and found out that they were so educated women. Solicitors, doctors, and everything. And so we had some contact already to what is going on. And eventually, we learned that, that because there was such bombardment that they never got the wings together with the body of the planes.

speaker: interviewee
text: But anyhow, we did our work. Night shift, day shift, 12 hours. It was a very heavy drill I had to hold. I had to learn you had to do it straight. And there was a horrible German in, in charge of us. He wasn't SS or anything, but he was horrible. He was always throwing a hammer. I mean, look, he had to do an important job, and he had slave labor. I mean, how could it all function? I mean.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the SS ladies, they were-- they were sort of in charge of us. But I've never been harmed. I have never been tortured. I have never been experimented on. And when we needed to go to the toilet at the factory, we had to go to the SS lady and ask if we can go to a toilet. And Hannelore, my girlfriend who was from Berlin and who now lives in Canada, she was also half-Jewish. And we had already a little bit hair. And both of us blonde with light eyes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we went to the SS lady and said, can we go to the toilet? And she said, but you are not Jews. No, we said. We are not. Our mothers are Aryans. It was so funny. That day she straight away picked us. And she helped us later on. She brought us some sugar when we were on the way for three weeks to Mauthausen. What were the living conditions like in Freiberg?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it, it-- if we wouldn't have had the bed bugs and the fleas, it was all right. I mean, you had a straw sack. You had your own bed. What I did any minute of my spare time, I slept. I wasn't talking to people. All I was like making energy. Wherever I could do, I slept. It wasn't so bad. OK, we had no soap. We had no toothpaste. We had no toothbrush. There was cold water to wash. And we were very hungry. And we were very cold. For me, being cold is worse than being hungry. And of course, it's a terrible job to stand for 12 hours. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you aware of the Allied advance at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, all we-- we, we didn't know if troops are coming or anything. But we knew when the planes-- there was only one time that all at once the Germans all ran away. They had a shelter under the-- under the factory. They closed the factory. And we said, what's going on? When we went to the window and the little silver planes and so many. And it was so wonderful because we, we knew that there is some hope. And that must have been January, February maybe. And when-- when-- they bombarded Dresden. And when I was in the night shift and I went to the-- home-- home to the barracks, we saw Dresden burning, you know. So we knew something is happening.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the effect of that on the SS?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. That-- nobody, nobody told us anything or-- I mean, we had no contact with the Germans. The Germans wouldn't come to us and say, you know, you caused it all. We felt pretty safe, actually, during the planes, because we were working. And so we didn't feel a danger at that time at all. But what happened is-- I think it was in, in, in, in March-- obviously, they couldn't get the wings anywhere at all. It was all finished. All the, the, the tracks were broken. The trains were standing all over the place. So I mean, in retrospect, now I know they decided we were going to Mauthausen to be gassed.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were all again loaded into trains, and they were open trains, and it was raining. And we had, I think, each one blanket and the blanket got heavier and heavier from the rain. And, and we had only one little cup-- sort of a tin cup. Once in a while we got coffee, but we had nowhere to go to the toilet, so we peed in our tin cup and threw it out of the train. And that-- I don't know how long we were in those open, oaken wagons. But eventually the Unterscharfuhrer Brown, who was all the time with us-- and I mean, OK, he was an SS man-- he, he probably wasn't sane-- but he tried each time to go us some soup, to get us some bread, and he got us the closed wagons, so that was sort of better. You know? But the train just didn't go anywhere. It stood here, it stood there. It went for an hour. It stood again. So the whole trip from Auschwitz-- from Freiberg to Mauthausen, which I think maybe should take four hours or five, took three weeks. For three weeks, we were in the train, and I mean cooped up, really. I don't think everybody could lie down at the same time. Some had to sit. Some had to-- had to lie down.

speaker: interviewee
text: And when we came to Mauthausen, I don't know-- I had some funny feeling. We knew we were in Austria, and I don't know if I had heard from Mauthausen, but you could see the Alps with the snow. And I had some recollection Linz is somewhere nearby, but what Mauthausen is, I don't know. Anyhow, they let us out of the train, and the train didn't go into the camp. The train went, like every train goes, into the village. Just near, near, near the village. And then we came all out. And we all had a big K and L on our backs. Koncentracionslager. Concentration camp. And we came out and we were so thirsty. And there, there was this spring in the middle of the village, sort of in the middle of the village. And we were all running with our little cups at-- to get some water. And there were some Germans sort of looking. And one said to the other, they are even worse off because they are not Jews. They will suffer even longer. Because we had no, no stars. We only had that we are from the concentration camp. And so I said, oh, well, that's not very good news.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we marched and marched. It's very-- quite a bit to go to, to the camp, uphill through the woods. And I thought, oh, should I run away? Shouldn't I? I didn't have the guts to run away, and I think the chances would have been pretty slim.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we came to this-- it looked like some Mickey Mouse castle. Quite-- everything was so unreal. It looked like a castle that has a drawbridge. I still don't know if it-- it's a drawbridge or, or-- it, it had a bridge-- first of all some, some buildings, and then there was a big bridge, and then you went to the rest of the-- of the camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we came there and, again, the boss. You know. So clean. Everything has to be nice and-- so they took us again. They took away our, our clothes. And I still had the photo and I did something very silly with the [? Buddha. ?] I put it down here and it fell out and went down the drain. So the last-- so just before the war I lost the [? Buddha. ?] And I kept the photo.

speaker: interviewee
text: I am under the impression that they didn't have clothes for women because they thought we are coming there, we are going in the gas chambers. They didn't prepare for us. But because the trains were not going, they didn't get the poisonous gas. And now they were stuck with 1,000 women, and they didn't know what to do. And I think that they gave us long johns. I really know-- I must try how to find out. But I really have the feeling that a thousand women-- and the moon was shining and we went across this drawbridge.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it must have looked so, so ridiculous, you know? So eventually, we went further, further into a building. Mauthausen is enormous. It's not as big as Auschwitz, but very big. And we came to the barrack and it had typhoid written on it. God, what now? And-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, the same. The same thing, that the-- you know, the beds above each other and so. And I, I don't know how long I was there. Three weeks, two weeks, I don't know. Next to us was a camp, and the men were like skeletons. And they came all running out, looking us over. And there was this one poor fellow. He was totally naked, had a bandaged penis and a belt around his waist. And in the belt he had a spoon. And he wasn't Czech or German. And he started talking to me, [LAUGHS] and I didn't-- I didn't want anything to do with him, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So then there were some Czech people there, also prisoners, not Jews, in the camp. And somehow they came also with messages. So I don't know what jobs they had. They could move freely. And they came to, to welcome us, like the Czech girls. So we got a little bit of feedback that, that things are-- unless the gas comes, that things are not so bad, that Americans are coming closer and closer.

speaker: interviewee
text: And on the 5th of May, we were told the whole SS-- they're all gone. They damaged the crematorium. They broke everything up. I don't know what-- they wanted to hide it, you know. So there was no more crematorium. The Germans had left, and the Americans came. It was just so wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember one came to me. And he did like that and he said, how come you're not skinny? And I wasn't skinny. I was exactly eight stone as I am today. How I did it-- I think I must have done it because I felt I'm not a victim, and I was looking from the outside in. And none of us were skeletons, really. But, but I, I, I was in, in pretty good shape. So then the Americans came. And they went into the village, and they got food, and they cooked the most wonderful soup that would make you all sick now. And they gave it to us, that soup, and half of the people died. Because it was so fat, and it was such a rich soup, I got such a diarrhea. I was so ill. We were all ill. And the, the thin people, of course, they died straight away. So there was no crematorium. What to do with all the dead people? They were piled up everywhere, and the stench is like flowers when you have them too long in the vase, a sweet smell. So the Americans got the Germans from the village, and they dug up the whole soccer place where the Germans had to play soccer. And they opened it all up, and there was this enormous mass grave. And, and they were throwing all the people in, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then they, they organized trains in different directions. All I cared for was the train going to Prague. And on the 19th of May, 1945, exactly on the day two years later, I came back to Prague.

speaker: interviewer
text: How was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: How it was to be back? Scary because my mother had disappeared because the Czechs were so enraged by the Germans that anybody who wasn't speaking perfectly Czech, they, they, they bashed them up. They murdered them. They hanged the people So my mother was frightened to stay in, in her flat. And when I came to our block of flats, I, I had to knock at the door. And I got the key and a letter that my mother was hidden [LAUGHS] somewhere in the country. And I had to go and get my mother back to, to Prague. And then I was, of course, protecting her, because they couldn't touch her with me-- my papers, you know, from the concentration camp. So she was all right as long as she didn't open her mouth.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the reaction of the people in Czechoslovakia to the returning?

speaker: interviewee
text: They didn't give a damn for the Jews. They never did. The Czechs never liked the Jews. I-- oh, there were-- some of the Jewish women actually were Communists. I can't understand it, because when we were-- when we were in those awful camps, some of the women always said, oh, I want to be liberated by a Russian. And my girlfriend was liberated by the Russian. They, they raped her and gave her syphilis. I always wanted to be liberated by the Americans, because, you know, I'm very pro-America and I'm very anti-communist. Very anti-communist. So.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you tell people what had happened to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. By the time-- that was nine months since they cut off our hair, so we all looked a little bit OK, you know, especially I had very curly hair. It looked all right. Nobody could see I come from some concentration camp. And I don't think my mother asked me much. And I-- we couldn't talk about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that we, we couldn't talk about it. I think nobody wanted to know. And I also think we thought if we say, nobody will believe us. So it, it was only when documentaries in the-- in the movies, and then [INAUDIBLE] came out with the piles of the-- of the bodies, and the, the hair of the women, and the piles of, of glasses, and artificial limbs. When you saw all that in the movies, people still didn't believe it. There are still people today who don't believe it all happened, you know? So eventually-- and you see, we didn't need to talk about it, because all of us were there. We didn't want to talk about it. We knew it. And I think that we sort of, again, mixed more or less together. All-- I mean-- there were people from the Israel side-- we, we all sort of found each other after the war. I don't know quite how. But the worst thing is when I came back, I was waiting, waiting, waiting. And no Goldie.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I started asking around. I, I, I tried to find out everything. And it took me 40 years or longer. I got a letter from the Jewish Museum, who said that the transport of the 1,000 people that he was in, they were all murdered in going toward towards Ossowa or Sobibor.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was the-- that was the saddest thing, that I went through all that awful mess without really-- it made no sense. But for me it makes sense, because I could never be so involved in helping exploited and abused animals if I wouldn't have been in that same situation. And I have given my whole life now to-- for many, many, many years, I work in animal welfare because I think that-- I, I think I learned an awful lot in the camp. And what about learning the fate of your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh-- I mean, you knew by the time-- right in Theresienstadt, I knew. Old people, they, they were the first to go from Theresienstadt. All I knew, that, that his transport went to Treblinka. My father must have been about 67 or sort of nearly as old as I am. And you know, people were much older at that time than we are now. So I mean, there was just no hope for-- I think anybody who would have been over 55, that, that there was no hope for them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how did you pick up the pieces of your life? Where did you go to live then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my mother still had the, the apartment. Not the elaborate that-- when I was born, but in the meantime we moved to a modern, smaller flat. And I learned how to make hand-woven materials. And some of the boys from Theresienstadt got back their farm and they had sheep, and they had wool and they supplied the wool to me. And I start-- starting having it dyed and making very nice materials. And because after the war there was nothing, it was terrific black market. Everything-- I mean, if, if you just little bit with it, you could make a very good living. And with those materials, I, I really did very well. Even if things improved in Prague, it was handmade. It looked very smart for, for skirts and jackets and things.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was doing very well and-- but-- well, it didn't last very long, because three years later Czechoslovakia turned Communist. And I left everything and took a suitcase. And with two friends-- one of Goldie's friends and my boyfriend at the time. We went to a German family near the border. And the borders were already closed. And at night, again, like thieves in the night, the three of us were guided by the Germans across into Bavaria. And it took us the whole night, because our guide lost the way.

speaker: interviewee
text: And eventually, daylight came, and we saw a cow that is like a cocker spaniel in two colors. Because in, in Prague, all cows are brown or black or something. And in Bavaria the cows are in two or three colors. And we saw this cow, and we knew we are in Bavaria.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we went onto the road and a Jeep came and picked us up and took us to a German camp, Regensburg. And we were I don't know how long in that camp. There wasn't-- they didn't have much food to give us. The Germans had no food. It's not that they wanted-- didn't want to give us food, but there was no food. But very soon we went to Italy and the camp was called Bagnoli in Italy.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that was lovely. The ocean-- the first time in my life I saw the ocean and all the Italian good food. Even if it was camp food, it was terrific, all that pasta. And I had a very good job in the camp. I worked in the, in the X-ray department. And I had a room with my girlfriend. I made friends with a girl who is now an old lady like me. And she's in Carmel. And we had a room together. And, and Italy was very good. But I had no connection to go to America. I had no relatives to send me papers.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we waited in the camp and then came Chile and Venezuela. And I didn't think that was right. And one day on the loudspeaker they said, Australia is opening migration. So all three of us went. And I came in 1949 to Sydney. And I've been here ever since. Stop.
